title: How two species can help each other survive

lesson.learn:
Symbiosis is a close relationship between species that live together or interact very closely. In mutualism, both partners benefit. One species may get food, shelter, or protection while the other gets nutrients, cleaning, or help with reproduction. These relationships are built into ecosystems everywhere.

The important part is that symbiosis is not rare decoration on top of nature. It is one of the ways ecosystems actually function. Many plants, animals, and fungi depend on partnerships that make survival easier for both sides.
lesson.simpler: Some species help each other. One gets a benefit, and the other does too. That is mutualism, a kind of symbiosis.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Symbiosis shows that survival in nature often depends on cooperation, not just competition.
lesson.deeperDive:
Classic examples include clownfish and sea anemones, where each partner gains protection or food access, and mycorrhizal fungi, which help plant roots absorb nutrients while receiving sugars in return. Pollinators also fit the pattern: plants get reproduction help, and animals get food.

These partnerships can be surprisingly specific. Over time, species may evolve traits that fit each other closely, making the relationship even more efficient.
lesson.funFact: Some plants and fungi share underground partnerships you never see.
references: USDA - mycorrhizal fungi and plant health

people: Marine biologists - study fish and anemone relationships
places: Coral reefs - clownfish mutualism is common here
concepts: Coevolution - partners shaping each other over time
